title: Prompt Engineering Isn't Magic
concept: Prompting as an empirical, testable discipline rather than folklore; the iterate-and-measure mindset.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: how do you force an AI to return its answer as structured JSON instead of a casual paragraph?

options.text: Explicitly instruct it with a schema or example of the exact JSON format you want
Just ask nicely and hope it picks JSON on its own
Use a longer prompt so the model has more room to decide the format

exercises.explain: To reliably get structured output like JSON, you give explicit format instructions — often including a schema or example — rather than leaving the format up to chance.

cards.title: No Secret Spell Required
cards.body: You've probably seen posts claiming 'say please and the AI works better!' or 'this one weird phrase unlocks genius answers.' Sounds like magic — but real prompt engineering is closer to science than spellcasting.

cards.title: An Empirical Discipline
cards.body: Prompt engineering means treating prompts like experiments: you form a guess about what wording will work, test it on real examples, measure the results, and change the prompt based on evidence. It's not about memorizing 'magic phrases.'

cards.title: Prompting is Like Recipe Testing
cards.body: A chef doesn't guess a recipe once and call it done. They cook it, taste it, tweak the salt, and cook it again. Prompt engineering works the same way: write, test, measure, adjust, repeat.

cards.title: Iterate and Measure in Action
cards.body: A team building a support bot tries a prompt, then runs it on 50 real customer questions and checks how many answers were correct and on-topic. They tweak the wording, run the 50 questions again, and compare the new score to the old one before deciding to keep the change.

cards.title: Myth: There's One Perfect Prompt
cards.body: Myth: somewhere out there is a magic phrase that fixes every prompt forever. Truth: the 'best' prompt depends on your model, your task, and your data — and it can stop working when the model updates. That's why testing beats trusting rumors.

cards.title: Folklore vs. Iteration
cards.body: One mindset trusts rumors and vibes; the other trusts tests and evidence. Only one of these scales as your prompts and tasks get more complex.

left.text: Believes a 'trick' works because it sounds convincing or went viral online

right.text: Tests the prompt on real examples, measures results, and keeps only what actually improves performance

exercises.prompt: Which approach best reflects prompt engineering as an empirical discipline?

options.text: Picking the prompt that 'feels' the most polite
Copying a prompt from a forum post because it went viral
Running two prompt versions on the same test cases and comparing measured results
Writing the longest, most detailed prompt possible every time

exercises.explain: Running two prompt versions on the same test cases and comparing the actual measured results is the core of empirical prompt engineering — you let evidence, not intuition or trends, decide which prompt wins.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Once you find a prompt that works well, it will keep working the same way forever, even after the underlying model is updated.
exercises.explain: False. Models get updated over time, and a prompt that worked perfectly before might behave differently afterward. That's why ongoing testing and measurement matter, not just a one-time check.

exercises.sentence: Good prompt engineers treat wording changes as experiments and measure the results instead of trusting internet folklore.
exercises.explain: Treating prompt changes as experiments means you test them against real examples and check the outcome, rather than assuming a trick works just because someone said it does.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three statements about prompt engineering was likely written by an AI trying to sound impressive but is actually shaky advice?

options.text: Always test your prompt on multiple real examples before trusting it in production.
This single magic phrase guarantees flawless AI output every time, for any model, forever.
Compare two prompt versions and measure which one performs better on your task.

exercises.explain: The claim of a 'magic phrase' that guarantees flawless results for any model, forever, is a red flag — it ignores that models differ, tasks differ, and models change over time. Real prompt engineering advice is always testable and specific, not a universal guarantee.

exercises.prompt: You're building an AI feature and someone says 'just use the magic prompt words everyone talks about online.' What's the empirically-minded response?

options.text: Trust the viral prompt because it worked for someone else's use case
Test the suggested prompt against your own examples, measure results, and only keep it if it actually performs better
Avoid testing since prompt behavior is too random to measure
Add every trending trick at once to maximize your chances

exercises.explain: Good prompt engineering treats every claim as a hypothesis, not a fact. A prompt that worked great for someone else's task might do nothing for yours — the only way to know is to run it on your own examples and measure the outcome. This test-and-measure mindset is what separates real prompt engineering from folklore, and it's the mindset you'll carry into every unit ahead as you build real LLM applications.
